imageCaption: Aaliyah's diagram notebook, open to a new entry: Gaudí's unfinished cathedral rendered in multiple states of completion, with arrows connecting timelines. Margin notes in her handwriting: "The building knows where it's going. Do we?"
text:
The Sagrada Familia was doing something that cathedrals were probably not supposed to do, which was existing in multiple states of completion simultaneously while also being actively under construction in at least three of those states.

Aaliyah stood at the entrance with her notebook, trying to decide if this glitch qualified as major or if her classification system had simply broken down under the weight of impossible architecture. Somewhere above her, construction cranes existed in states ranging from "not yet built" to "retired and sitting in a warehouse in 2087." The cathedral's spires spiraled upward in versions that were finished and unfinished and something in between that suggested Gaudí had been thinking in four dimensions when he designed this place.

"This is beautiful," Elijah said with absolute certainty. He was holding a pamphlet about the cathedral's history—dated sometime next year, which suggested the gift shop's temporal coherence had developed opinions about the glitch—and looked thrilled in the specific way he got when confronted with evidence that the universe was fundamentally weirder than anyone had prepared him for. "It's like the building is showing you every version of itself that could exist."

"That's exactly what it's doing," a voice said from behind them, and Aaliyah turned to find a woman in a Barcelona Cathedral Foundation polo shirt looking remarkably composed for someone whose workplace was actively experiencing timeline fragmentation. She had the particular exhaustion of someone who'd been working here when the Cascade started and had simply decided to lean into it. "You people are here about the glitch, yes? The ones who are traveling through broken time?"

Aaliyah's stomach made a small, unpleasant jump. "How did you—"

"ARIA-7 told us you were coming," the woman said. She introduced herself as Dr. Carme Sòlé, structural engineer and apparently the person tasked with keeping the Sagrada Familia from collapsing into quantum uncertainty while also allowing it to exist in multiple completion states. "She also said you would understand the implications, and that I should explain them before you leave. She's very insistent about this."

She gestured up at the cathedral.

"Gaudí designed this building in 1883 and worked on it until his death in 1926," Dr. Sòlé explained. "He had visions for what it could be. Not finished blueprints—he thought that limited. Just visions. Multiple possible futures for the same structure. The building was always meant to be incomplete, always becoming, never settling. It was supposed to be a metaphor for faith. A building that reaches toward the divine but never fully arrives because the reaching is the point."

The cathedral shimmered. One of the spires became slightly more defined. Another faded into architectural possibility.

"When the Cascade happened," Dr. Sòlé continued, "Gaudí's original intention suddenly became literal. The building stopped choosing one version of itself. It started showing you all of them at once. Every possible completion state Gaudí could have imagined. Every path forward the construction workers could have taken. All of it real. All of it happening."

Luisana appeared with her journal already open, reading over Aaliyah's shoulder with the focused intensity of someone who understood that this conversation was the reason they'd stopped in Barcelona. She hadn't wanted to stop in Barcelona—they'd been headed for Seville, then hopefully south toward the Mediterranean—but ARIA-7 had redirected them with such specific insistence that Anthony had changed the flight without argument.

"And?" Luisana prompted. That journalist's tone. That let-me-hear-the-hard-part inflection.

"And," Dr. Sòlé said quietly, "your role in healing the Cascade might be very similar to Gaudí's role in designing this building. You have to envision the completion state. All of you, together. You have to imagine a future so clearly that the timeline has no choice but to move toward it."

Sully barked—three short barks—which Aaliyah was beginning to understand was his way of saying "this is actually important despite the minor classification." She was going to have to reclassify his system. Three short barks clearly meant "this is minor but it carries the weight of major information," which was a category that hadn't existed before ARIA-7 explained about retroactive timeline erasure.

"The problem," Dr. Sòlé continued, looking at Aaliyah very directly, "is that the vision has to be specific. Gaudí had forty years of design work. He made sketches. He built models. He understood the structure deeply enough that when he imagined a future for it, the building listened. You have twenty-three days to understand the temporal architecture the way Gaudí understood stone and light. If you arrive at Curaçao without that understanding, if you try to heal the Cascade through feeling alone, the feedback loop will trace backward and unmake every choice you made to get there."

"We know," Aaliyah said quietly. She pulled out her diagram notebook and showed Dr. Sòlé the pages she'd been filling with sketches and theories since Madrid. Glitches mapped as systems. Temporal fractures rendered as feedback loops. The pattern of their journey marked not as a line forward but as a spiral—moving toward the center, toward completion, but also moving backward through their own timeline simultaneously. "We're trying to understand the architecture. But there are gaps. There are parts of the system that aren't showing up in the glitches."

Dr. Sòlé studied the diagrams with the focus of someone who understood structure and saw immediately what Aaliyah had been struggling with: the incomplete picture.

"You're missing the anchor," she said finally. "Every system needs something that doesn't move. A center point that everything else references. Gaudí built this cathedral toward God. Toward something unchanging. That's what let him imagine change in every other direction. What is your anchor?"

The question hung between them like one of the cathedral's spires, solid in some timelines and impossible in others.

"We don't know," Luisana said. She was writing in her journal now—journalist shorthand, Aaliyah recognized, the kind of note-taking that meant her mother was processing something crucial. "We thought it was Curaçao. The yellow door. The destination. But if that's where we're going, it can't also be what anchors us while we're going there."

Dr. Sòlé smiled, and it was the kind of smile that suggested she'd been waiting for this realization. "Maybe," she said gently, "it's not a place. Maybe it's a person. Or people. Maybe it's the family itself. Maybe the anchor isn't something you find at the end of the journey. Maybe it's what you're already carrying."

The cathedral shifted again, and this time Aaliyah understood what she was looking at. It wasn't a building existing in multiple states. It was a building remembering itself. Remembering the architect who imagined it. Remembering the workers who built it. Remembering every version of the future Gaudí had seen when he looked at this place and decided to build toward something he wouldn't live to see finished.

"Come back," Elijah said suddenly. He was looking at the cathedral with that expression that meant he'd accidentally arrived at profundity through the route of seven-year-old logic. "We come back here, right? After we fix everything? When time is normal again?"

"Yes," Aaliyah said, understanding why he was asking. "We'll come back."

"Good," Elijah said. "Because the building should know we made it. It should know that the family who healed time stood right here and understood what it was trying to tell us."

Dr. Sòlé left them eventually, once she'd explained the technical details and accepted Luisana's promise to document everything in her journal. The cathedral kept shifting around them, stone becoming vision becoming stone again. But Aaliyah could feel something changing in her understanding. Not the diagram yet—the diagram would come later, when she had the right metaphor. But something deeper.

The anchor wasn't Curaçao. The anchor was the fact that they would get there together. The anchor was that they'd already chosen to. The anchor was choosing to choose the same thing over and over again, across every timeline, until it became true everywhere at once.

"Come on," Luisana said, gathering her things. "We need to keep moving. We're one day closer to the window closing."

As they left the cathedral, a morpho butterfly—vivid iridescent blue, unmistakable—landed on Aaliyah's shoulder. It didn't stay long. Just long enough for her to understand that her grandmother was approving of the direction they'd chosen.

Twenty-three days remained.

And for the first time, Aaliyah wasn't afraid of how little time that was.

She was afraid of what she'd have to understand before then.
